title: ECA’s first LNG cargo departs amid feedgas disruption signals | Wood Mackenzie
gist: Mexico's Energía Costa Azul LNG facility shipped its first fully loaded export cargo on 7 July 2026, but satellite imagery, pipeline nominations and feedgas data show liquefaction activity slowing sharply days before and after, driven by a Las Dunas compressor station outage and gas-quality issues.

insights.claim: Mexico's Energía Costa Azul (ECA) LNG project shipped its first LNG export cargo on the Pacific Success, which arrived 6 July 2026, departed Ensenada 7 July, and was confirmed by Sempra Infrastructure as ECA's first export on 8 July 2026.
insights.speaker: Ricardo Falcón-Bautista
insights.so_what: Marks the operational start of Mexico's Pacific-coast LNG export sector, a new supply source relevant to Asian/global LNG balance and Sempra Infrastructure's project economics.
insights.quote: The vessel's draft increased from 9.4 metres on arrival to 11.5 metres on departure, equivalent to 96% of its summer load maximum.

themes.theme: Feedgas disruption signals ahead of the milestone

insights.claim: Wood Mackenzie satellite imagery showed ECA's liquefaction activity slowing from gas day 2 July 2026, with no visible heating signatures observed from 3 July onward, indicating a facility slowdown that preceded the official first-cargo announcement.
insights.speaker: Ricardo Falcón-Bautista
insights.so_what: Demonstrates that satellite-based liquefaction monitoring can flag operational shifts at LNG facilities days ahead of company disclosures, valuable for LNG traders positioning around cargo timing.

insights.claim: Gasoducto Rosarito (GRO) pipeline nominations for ECA Liquefaction fell from over 400 MMcf/d (two-week average) to just 31 MMcf on 2 July and 7 MMcf on 4 July 2026, coinciding with the liquefaction slowdown.
insights.speaker: Ricardo Falcón-Bautista
insights.so_what: Sharp pipeline nomination declines are a leading indicator of feedgas curtailment that traders can monitor to anticipate LNG cargo delays or facility outages.

insights.claim: GRO reported scheduled maintenance at the Las Dunas compressor station running through 10 July 2026 and flagged gas quality issues at ECA's regasification receipt point on 1 and 6 July, causing feedgas deliveries (previously exceeding 400 MMcf/d) to fall to zero from 5-7 July.
insights.speaker: Ricardo Falcón-Bautista
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific mechanical cause (compressor maintenance plus gas-quality issues) behind ECA's feedgas interruption, useful for assessing near-term reliability of Mexico's new LNG export capacity.

insights.claim: California pipeline exports of gas to Mexico dropped from a two-week average of 828 MMcf/d to below 500 MMcf/d after 2 July 2026, coinciding with the ECA feedgas disruption.
insights.speaker: Ricardo Falcón-Bautista
insights.so_what: Cross-border gas flow data confirms the disruption's scale reaches upstream into US export pipelines, relevant to US Gulf/West Coast gas balance forecasting.

themes.theme: Value of alternative-data monitoring for LNG markets

insights.claim: Combining vessel tracking, satellite monitoring and pipeline flow analytics can reveal operational changes at LNG facilities often days before official company announcements, a capability increasingly valuable as Mexico's LNG export sector expands.
insights.speaker: Ricardo Falcón-Bautista
insights.so_what: Frames a repeatable monitoring methodology investors/traders can apply to other nascent LNG export projects to front-run official disclosures.
insights.quote: The episode demonstrates how vessel tracking, satellite monitoring and pipeline analytics can provide an early indication of operational changes, often days before official announcements.
